text: Sigiriya is an ancient rock fortress in Sri Lanka built by King Kashyapa in the 5th century AD and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
King Kashyapa chose Sigiriya as his capital after seizing the throne from his father, King Dhatusena.
After King Kashyapa's death, Sigiriya was transformed into a Buddhist monastery.
Sigiriya is often referred to as the Eighth Wonder of the World due to its historical and architectural significance.
The site shows evidence of human settlement even before King Kashyapa's reign.
Sigiriya rises approximately 200 meters above the surrounding plains.
The Lion’s Paw is the entrance staircase carved into the rock in the shape of a lion.
The summit of Sigiriya contains ruins of an ancient royal palace.
Sigiriya is designed with symmetrical layouts showing advanced urban planning.
Massive staircases and terraces were carved directly into the rock surface.
Sigiriya contains one of the oldest hydraulic systems in the world.
Water gardens at Sigiriya use underground channels and surface reservoirs.
Ancient engineers created fountains that still function during rainy seasons.
The site demonstrates advanced knowledge of water pressure control.
Symmetrical garden layouts reflect careful engineering and planning.
Sigiriya frescoes depict celestial maidens painted on the western rock face.
The frescoes are considered masterpieces of ancient Sri Lankan art.
Only a small portion of the original frescoes remains today.
The paintings show advanced use of natural pigments and shading techniques.
The identity of the women in the frescoes is still debated by historians.
The Mirror Wall was once polished so highly that it reflected images like a mirror.
The Mirror Wall contains ancient graffiti written by visitors over centuries.
Graffiti includes poems, comments, and praises written in early Sinhala script.
Sigiriya reflects a combination of royal power, art, and Buddhist influence.
The site represents ancient Sri Lankan civilization and cultural identity.
Sigiriya is located in the Matale District of Sri Lanka.
It attracts thousands of local and foreign tourists every year.
The rock is made of hardened magma from an extinct volcano.
Sigiriya is one of the best-preserved ancient urban planning sites in Asia.
It is listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1982.
The main entrance to Sigiriya was designed in the shape of a giant lion.
The Lion’s Paw remains as two large carved stone feet at the entrance.
The site includes symmetrical gardens divided into water, boulder, and terraced gardens.
Sigiriya gardens are aligned with geometric precision and water flow systems.
Underground drainage systems prevent water damage to structures.
The site was rediscovered in the 19th century by British archaeologists.
Excavations revealed hidden gardens and palace foundations.
Sigiriya is a symbol of ancient Sri Lankan innovation and creativity.
The site blends natural landscape with human engineering.
The climb to Sigiriya summit involves over 1,200 steps.
Sigiriya is one of the most visited tourist attractions in Sri Lanka.
The frescoes are painted on a sheltered rock surface protected from weather.
Only a small group of frescoes remains due to natural erosion and vandalism.
Cooling and ventilation techniques were used in the palace design.
The site demonstrates early use of landscape architecture principles.
Sigiriya was built as both a fortress and a royal pleasure garden.
The site reflects the transition between monarchy and monastic use.
The surrounding area includes wildlife and forest ecosystems.
Sigiriya is part of Sri Lanka’s Cultural Triangle.
Stone pathways and staircases were carved to blend with natural rock formations.
The irrigation system allowed gardens to function even in dry seasons.
Sigiriya remains a major symbol of Sri Lankan heritage identity.
The artistic style shows influence of South Asian classical painting traditions.
Sigiriya represents one of the earliest examples of planned city design in the region.
The site is protected by the Department of Archaeology of Sri Lanka.
